_teamNote: Add new React Challenge questions by copying this object format. Do not use // comments because this file must stay valid JSON. WILL NEED TO GET QUESTIONS FROM CURRICULUM DEPARTMENT
prompt: Your grocery cart is $12 over budget. Which swap helps most?
choices: Trade bottled water for a reusable bottle
Buy the same snacks in smaller bags
explanation: A reusable bottle cuts a repeated cost without removing a need.

prompt: A subscription renews tomorrow, but you have not used it in 3 months.
explanation: Canceling unused recurring charges protects future cash flow.

prompt: You get paid Friday and rent is due Monday. What should happen first?
choices: Leave the full check in spending money
explanation: Needs with fixed due dates should be protected before wants.

prompt: Two items are on sale. One is a planned need, one is an impulse want.
choices: Buy both because they are discounted
Buy the want before the sale ends
explanation: A sale only saves money when the purchase was already useful.

prompt: You have $25 left for the week. Which choice is strongest?
explanation: Planning plus a small cushion makes the money last longer.

prompt: Your phone bill is higher than expected. What is the smartest first move?
choices: Review charges and remove unused add-ons
Ignore the bill until next month
Buy a newer phone on credit
explanation: Checking the bill can reveal fees or services you can remove immediately.

prompt: You want takeout, but groceries are already at home. Which choice protects your budget?
choices: Cook tonight and save takeout for a planned day
Order delivery because it feels easier
explanation: Using food you already paid for avoids stacking an extra want on top.

prompt: A friend invites you to an expensive event. What response fits a budget plan?
choices: Put the full cost on a card without checking
explanation: Offering a lower-cost alternative keeps the friendship and the budget alive.

prompt: Your emergency fund is empty. Where should extra money go first?
explanation: Even a small cushion makes surprise costs less damaging.

prompt: You found a coupon for an item you do not need. What is the best call?
choices: Buy it because the coupon expires
Buy two to maximize the discount
explanation: A discount on an unnecessary item is still extra spending.

prompt: Your budget has categories for needs, wants, and savings. Which item belongs in needs?
explanation: Utilities are essential costs, while merch and upgrades are wants.

prompt: You are comparing two cereal boxes. Which detail helps find the better value?
explanation: Unit price lets you compare value even when package sizes differ.

prompt: How many months of expenses do experts usually suggest for an emergency fund?
explanation: Three to six months of essential expenses is the common target for a full emergency fund.

prompt: Your credit card bill arrives. What protects your credit score most?
choices: Paying at least the minimum on time
explanation: Payment history is the biggest factor in a credit score, so on-time payments matter most.

prompt: A friend offers a 'guaranteed double your money in a week' deal. What is the smartest move?
choices: Walk away, because guaranteed high returns are a scam signal
explanation: No legitimate investment guarantees fast, high returns. That promise is a classic scam pattern.

prompt: You get your first paycheck and it is smaller than expected. Why?
choices: The bank charged a deposit fee
explanation: Gross pay shrinks to net pay after taxes and deductions are withheld.

prompt: Which choice spreads out investment risk the most?
choices: All savings in one exciting stock
A diversified fund holding many companies
explanation: Diversification lowers the damage any single company can do to your money.

prompt: Holiday gifts cost you about $120 every December. What is the smoothest plan?
choices: Put it all on credit in December
Save $10 a month in a sinking fund
Skip gifts if money is tight
explanation: A sinking fund turns a known yearly cost into a small, painless monthly habit.

prompt: Two loans are identical except one has a higher APR. What does the higher APR mean?
choices: It has a longer grace period
explanation: APR is the yearly cost of borrowing, so higher APR means a more expensive loan.

prompt: Choosing the concert ticket means skipping the new game. That tradeoff is called:
explanation: Opportunity cost is the value of the next-best option you give up when you choose.

prompt: Why does starting to save at 15 beat starting at 25, even with smaller amounts?
choices: Compound growth has more years to work
Prices were lower in the past
explanation: Compounding builds on itself, so extra years matter more than extra dollars.

prompt: A text says your bank account is locked and asks you to click a link fast. You should:
choices: Ignore the link and check the official app directly
explanation: Urgency plus a link is a phishing pattern. Always go to the official app or site yourself.

prompt: Your debit card is different from a credit card because a debit card:
choices: Lets you borrow from the bank
explanation: Debit pulls directly from your checking account, while credit borrows money you repay later.

prompt: Rent, insurance, and a phone plan are examples of:
explanation: Fixed expenses stay about the same every month, which makes them easy to plan around.

prompt: You want a $60 game in 3 months. Which plan actually gets you there?
choices: Buy now and owe a friend
explanation: Breaking a goal into a monthly amount turns wishing into a plan.

prompt: An item is 'Buy one get one 50% off.' What should you check before grabbing it?
choices: Whether you needed two in the first place
How big the sale sign is
explanation: Deals only save money when they cover something you already planned to buy.
